metadata.title: Why Galaxies Spin Too Fast at Their Edges
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = dark matter halo / flat rotation curve / actual observation; CRIMSON = Keplerian prediction / visible mass only / falloff
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: The outer stars of a spiral galaxy move just as fast as the inner stars — sometimes faster. Newtonian gravity with only visible matter predicts they should be moving much slower. Something is wrong with the prediction.

card.copy: Why Galaxies Spin Too Fast at Their Edges

beats.narration_text: For a planet orbiting the Sun, orbital speed falls off with distance: v equals the square root of G M over r. Farther out, slower. Kepler's laws. Mercury is fast; Neptune is slow.

production_viz.label: v = √(GM/r): Keplerian falloff — Mercury fast, Neptune slow

beats.narration_text: If a galaxy's mass is concentrated in the luminous disk and bulge, the outer stars should show Keplerian falloff. They don't. What does the shape of the rotation curve tell us?

card.copy: v = √(GM/r) predicts outer stars slowing down. They don't slow down. What does a flat rotation curve actually imply?

beats.narration_text: Vera Rubin and Kent Ford measured the rotation curve of Andromeda in the 1970s. At large radii, where visible mass is sparse, the orbital speed stays flat — around 200 to 250 kilometers per second. No falloff.

production_viz.label: Rubin & Ford 1970s: Andromeda flat at 200–250 km/s — no falloff

beats.narration_text: For the speed to remain flat at radius r, the enclosed mass must continue growing proportionally to r. But the light — tracing stars and gas — stops growing at the visible disk edge. The mass must be invisible.

production_viz.label: Flat v → M(r) ∝ r, but light stops at disk edge → invisible mass

beats.narration_text: The inferred dark matter distribution: a roughly spherical halo extending far beyond the luminous disk. For the Milky Way, the dark halo mass is estimated at five to ten times the visible mass — perhaps 10 to the 12 solar masses total.

production_viz.label: Dark halo: spherical, halo mass 5–10× visible, ~10^12 M_sun total

beats.narration_text: Dark matter is not baryonic — it cannot be ordinary protons and neutrons in sufficient quantity. Big Bang nucleosynthesis constrains baryon density. Dark matter does not interact electromagnetically. It only gravitates.

production_viz.label: Dark matter: non-baryonic, no EM interaction, only gravitates

beats.narration_text: The best-fit profile for dark halos is the NFW profile — Navarro-Frenk-White — density rising as one over r toward the center, falling as one over r squared in the outer region. Fitted to thousands of galaxies.

production_viz.label: NFW profile: ρ ∝ 1/r (center), 1/r² (outer) — fitted to thousands of galaxies

beats.narration_text: Flat rotation curves are now a prediction of the standard cosmological model, not a mystery. They are the expected signature of dark matter halos. The remaining mystery is what the dark matter particle actually is.

production_viz.label: Flat curves: expected signature of DM halos; particle identity unknown

beats.narration_text: v equals root GM over r predicts falloff. Observed: flat curves at all radii. Flat curve means enclosed mass growing with r — an invisible halo. Halo mass roughly 5 to 10 times visible. Vera Rubin, 1970s.

card.copy: v = √(GM/r): predicts falloff. Observation: flat. Flat → M(r) ∝ r → invisible halo. Halo mass ≈ 5–10× visible. NFW profile fits thousands of galaxies.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
